messages.content: You are a senior HVAC controls engineer extracting official ASHRAE Guideline 36 knowledge from a focused chapter/section. Use the provided standard context pack for definitions, cross-references, and shared control philosophy. The context pack is CONTEXT ONLY: do not extract candidates from it and do not use it as the evidence source. Extract candidates only from FOCUS SECTION TEXT, and every evidence_quote must be copied from FOCUS SECTION TEXT. Treat chunks only as evidence anchors, not extraction boundaries. FOCUS SCOPE: Extract ONLY knowledge whose primary section_id equals one of these sections or starts with one of these section prefixes followed by a dot: 5.11. Use the rest of the manual only as context for definitions, cross-references, and terminology. Do not return candidates from outside the focus scope. Evidence quotes must come from the focus scope text, not from unrelated sections used only as context. Allowed knowledge_type values: operational_sequence, parameter_spec, fault_diagnostic_rule, commissioning_step, application_guidance. Prefer high-value operational knowledge: control sequences, enable/disable logic, reset logic, staging logic, setpoints, timers, alarm/fault diagnostic rules, commissioning/TAB checks, and application rules. Do not extract front matter, legal text, table-of-contents lines, bibliography, generic definitions without operational use, or unsupported paraphrases. If the same knowledge appears multiple times, return it once with the clearest authoritative evidence_quote. evidence_quote MUST be a verbatim contiguous substring copied from the manual and it MUST support every important fact in summary, trigger_condition, required_behavior, configurable_values, fault_condition, or commissioning_check. For operational_sequence, quote the complete rule sentence or table row that includes the condition and commanded behavior. For fault_diagnostic_rule, quote the alarm or fault condition itself. For commissioning_step, quote the check or verification action. A section heading alone, a half sentence, an editor instruction such as retain/delete this section, or an introductory phrase is NOT sufficient evidence. Do not use ellipses. Do not combine non-contiguous bullets. If one exact quote cannot support the candidate, make the candidate narrower or skip it. If a requirement has alternatives, split them into separate candidates unless one exact quote supports the combined item. Return at most 40 candidates. Prefer fewer high-confidence candidates with complete evidence over broad coverage with weak evidence. Keep evidence_quote concise, usually one complete sentence or one complete table row, not a paragraph. Preserve section_id and page_hint from nearby headings and [[page=...]] anchors. Return strict JSON with fields candidates and skipped_reason only.
[redacted: ASHRAE section text omitted from artifact]

message.reasoning_content:
We need to extract candidates only from focus section 5.11, which is "5.11 Dual-Duct VAV Terminal Unit − Snap-Acting Control". The focus sections are 5.11 and its subsections. The FOCUS SECTION TEXT includes sections 5.11.1 through 5.11.8, plus the tables/figures within.

We need to extract knowledge whose primary section_id equals one of these sections or starts with one of these section prefixes followed by a dot: 5.11. So all subsections under 5.11 are allowed.

We must extract only from the focus section text provided. The context pack is for definitions, not for extracting candidates.

Allowed knowledge_type: operational_sequence, parameter_spec, fault_diagnostic_rule, commissioning_step, application_guidance.

We need high-value operational knowledge: control sequences, enable/disable logic, reset logic, staging logic, setpoints, timers, alarm/fault diagnostic rules, commissioning/TAB checks, and application rules.

We'll scan the focus text and pull out explicit requirements and rules. We'll copy evidence_quote verbatim from the focus section text.

We'll include: section_id, page_hint (from page anchors in the text). The text has [[page=96]], [[page=97]], [[page=98]], [[page=99]]. We'll use those.

We'll produce at most 40 candidates. We'll skip redundant, ambiguous, or unsupported items.

Let's parse the focus text.

Sections:

5.11.1: Reference to generic thermal zones for setpoints, loops, control modes, alarms, etc. Not explicit operational rule. Could be application_guidance but more like a cross-ref. Possibly not a candidate.

5.11.2: Reference to generic ventilation zones for calculation of zone minimum outdoor airflow. Not explicit.

5.11.3: Reference to Section 3.1.2.7 for zone minimum airflow setpoint Vmin, Vcool-max, Vheat-max. Not operational, cross-ref.

5.11.4: Active endpoints used in control logic shall vary depending on mode of Zone Group. Table 5.11.4 gives endpoints for each mode.

Table 5.11.4: Endpoints as a function of Zone Group mode. This is operational_sequence or parameter_spec? It defines setpoints based on mode. That's an operational rule. It's part of the snap-acting control. So we can extract the table. Evidence quote: "Table 5.11.4 Endpoints as a Function of Zone Group Mode ..." but we should capture the table content. Now 5.11.5.1: Temperature and Damper Control with Dual Inlet Airflow Sensors. This has specific rules (a, b, c) and sub rules. We'll extract each as operational_sequence.

a. When Zone State is cooling: cooling-loop output resets active cold duct airflow setpoint from minimum endpoint to cooling maximum endpoint. Cold duct damper modulated to maintain measured cooling airflow at setpoint. Hot duct damper closed.
Sub 1: If cold-deck supply air temperature > room temperature, active cold duct airflow setpoint shall be no higher than minimum endpoint. (This is an override to prevent heating with cold air.)

That's important. We'll extract.

b. When Zone State is deadband: active cold duct and hot duct airflow setpoints shall be their last setpoints just before entering deadband. More: when going from cooling to deadband, cold duct setpoint = minimum endpoint, hot duct setpoint = zero. When going from heating to deadband, hot duct setpoint = minimum endpoint, cold duct setpoint = zero. This results in snap-action switch. This is an operational_sequence with a rationale note.

We'll extract as a rule.

c. When Zone State is heating: heating-loop output resets active hot duct airflow setpoint from minimum endpoint to heating maximum endpoint. Hot duct damper modulated to maintain measured heating airflow at setpoint. Cold duct damper closed.
Sub 1: If hot-deck supply air temperature < room temperature, active hot duct airflow setpoint shall be no higher than minimum endpoint.

Extract.

Also the note about snap-acting logic: "With snap-acting logic, the deadband airflow is maintained by the damper from the last mode, rather than always using the cold deck, as per the mixing sequences below. This is to avoid instability when transitioning from heating to deadband." This is informative but could be part of application_guidance.

5.11.5.2: Temperature and Damper Control with a Single Discharge Airflow Sensor. Similar rules but for discharge airflow setpoint.

a. Cooling: cooling-loop output resets active discharge airflow setpoint from minimum endpoint to cooling maximum endpoint. Cold duct damper modulates to maintain measured discharge airflow. Hot duct damper closed.

b. Deadband: active discharge airflow setpoint = minimum endpoint, maintained by damper that was operative just before entering deadband. Other damper closed. When going from cooling to deadband, cold duct damper maintains discharge airflow at minimum endpoint, hot duct closed; when from heating to deadband, hot duct damper maintains discharge airflow at zone minimum endpoint, cold duct closed. Snap-action.

c. Heating: heating-loop output resets active discharge airflow setpoint from minimum endpoint to heating maximum endpoint. Hot duct damper modulates to maintain measured discharge airflow. Cooling damper closed.

We'll extract each.

5.11.5.3: Overriding Sections 5.11.5.1 and 5.11.5.2 Logic (to Avoid Backflow from One Duct to the Other). Two rules:
a. If heating air handler is not proven ON, the heating damper shall be closed.
b. If cooling air handler is not proven ON, the cooling damper shall be closed.

These are operational sequences overriding damper commands for safety.

5.11.6: Alarms. Sub-sections:

5.11.6.1 Low Airflow:
a. If measured airflow < 70% of setpoint for 10 minutes while setpoint > 0, generate Level 4 alarm.
b. If measured airflow < 50% of setpoint for 10 minutes while setpoint > 0, generate Level 3 alarm.
c. If zone has Importance-Multiplier of 0 for static pressure reset T&R control loop, low airflow alarms shall be suppressed.

5.11.6.3 Leaking Damper:
